title: Holi – Festival of Colors
intro: Holi, also known as the Festival of Colors, is one of the most popular Hindu festivals celebrated across India and many parts of the world.
description:
Holi, also known as the Festival of Colors, is one of the most popular Hindu festivals celebrated across India and many parts of the world. It is observed on the full moon day (Purnima) of the Phalguna month, usually falling in February or March. The festival marks the arrival of spring, the end of winter, and the triumph of good over evil.

According to Hindu traditions, Holi is associated with the legend of Prahlada, Hiranyakashipu, and Holika. It symbolizes the victory of devotion and righteousness over arrogance and evil. Holi is also closely connected with the divine love of Lord Krishna and Radha, especially in Braj, Mathura, and Vrindavan, where the festival is celebrated with great enthusiasm.

People celebrate Holi by lighting the sacred Holika Dahan fire on the eve of the festival, playing with colors, singing, dancing, sharing sweets, and strengthening relationships with family and friends. Holi spreads the message of love, unity, forgiveness, and happiness.

Festival Month

Phalguna (February – March)

Significance / Importance

Holi holds great religious, cultural, and social importance in Hinduism. It celebrates the victory of good over evil through the story of Prahlada and Holika. The festival also welcomes the spring season and symbolizes new beginnings, joy, and positive energy.

In many regions, Holi is celebrated as the festival of Lord Krishna and Radha. Devotees participate in devotional songs, temple celebrations, and colorful festivities. The festival encourages people to forgive past differences, strengthen relationships, and celebrate together in harmony.

Puja Vidhi

1. Clean the home and prepare for Holika Dahan.
2. Worship the Holika bonfire with devotion.
3. Offer coconut, wheat ears, gram, flowers, and sacred threads.
4. Circumambulate (Parikrama) the Holika fire.
5. Pray for the removal of negativity and protection from evil.
6. The next day, celebrate Holi by applying colors with love and respect.
7. Visit family, friends, and temples to seek blessings.
8. Distribute sweets and celebrate with joy.

Related Katha / Mantra / Aarti / Chalisa

* Holika Dahan Katha
* Prahlada Katha
* Shri Krishna Mantra
* Shri Radha Krishna Aarti
* Shri Krishna Chalisa
* Shri Radha Chalisa

faq.a1: Holi is celebrated on the full moon day (Purnima) of the Phalguna month.
faq.a2: Holi celebrates the victory of Prahlada’s devotion over evil and marks the arrival of spring.
faq.a3: Holika Dahan is the sacred bonfire lit on the night before Holi, symbolizing the destruction of evil.
faq.q4: In which month is Holi celebrated?
faq.a4: Holi usually falls in February or March.
faq.q5: Why is Holi called the Festival of Colors?
faq.a5: It is called the Festival of Colors because people celebrate by joyfully applying colorful powders and water to one another.
faq.q6: Which deity is associated with Holi?
faq.a6: Holi is associated with Lord Krishna, Radha, and the story of Prahlada.
faq.q7: What are the main traditions of Holi?
faq.a7: Holika Dahan, playing with colors, offering prayers, sharing sweets, and celebrating with loved ones are the main traditions.
faq.q8: What is the spiritual significance of Holi?
faq.a8: Holi symbolizes the victory of good over evil, devotion over arrogance, and the celebration of love, unity, and new beginnings.
